skill.name: shirushi
skill.description: Document ID management for Git repositories. Validates, assigns, and tracks immutable doc_ids across Markdown/YAML files. Use when working with doc_id, document IDs, "@see docid" references, document integrity validation, or shirushi commands.
skill.summary: Manage immutable document IDs and references across Markdown and YAML files in Git repositories.
skill.author: CAPHTECH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick examples as Ruby or shell execution, but the file is Markdown documentation for a CLI workflow. No malicious intent, network access, credential handling, or prompt injection was found. Risk remains medium because the skill directs agents to run external commands that can inspect and modify repository files.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Can Modify Repository Files
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run shirushi commands. Validation and display commands appear safe, but assign and rehash workflows can change document metadata or content hashes in the working repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant commands are explicitly documented and their described purpose includes assigning IDs and recalculating hashes. This is legitimate for the skill, but it creates filesystem change risk when an agent runs the commands.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged inline backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell execution. In context, these are documentation examples for the shirushi CLI, not executable code embedded in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only file is Markdown, and the flagged lines are table entries or fenced command examples. There is no Ruby code, subprocess wrapper, or dynamic command construction.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the description line, but that line only describes document ID management. The checksum configuration later names mod26AZ, which is an identifier check digit, not a security control.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited description contains no cryptographic algorithm. The only algorithm-like text is presented as a check digit for IDs, so the weak crypto concern is not a confirmed security vulnerability.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Domain-Specific Lookups
low_findings.description: The flagged show, scan, and reference examples operate on document metadata within the repository. No evidence found of host enumeration, credential discovery, process listing, or other system reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command names are used in the context of document ID lookup and repository document listing. The file contains no commands that inspect the operating system or external environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Run External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on an external shirushi executable for linting, scanning, assigning IDs, and recalculating hashes. Agents should verify the executable and review diffs after mutating commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dependency on an external CLI is clear from the command table. The risk depends on the installed executable and repository context, so it is a controlled but real operational concern.

content.value_statement: Teams lose trust in documentation when IDs drift, references break, or metadata changes without review. Shirushi gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for validating, assigning, and tracking immutable document IDs.
actual_capabilities: Validate doc_id values and index integrity with shirushi lint.
List repository documents and metadata with shirushi scan.
Look up a document by immutable doc_id with shirushi show.
Assign document IDs to new Markdown or YAML files.
Recalculate content hashes and check doc_id references.
limitations: Requires the shirushi CLI to be installed and available in the repository environment.
Only covers repositories that use the documented Shirushi configuration and frontmatter format.
Mutating commands can change files and should be reviewed with Git diffs.
Does not provide the Shirushi implementation or CI configuration by itself.

use_cases.title: Validate documentation before merge
use_cases.description: Run doc_id and reference checks before a pull request is merged.

use_cases.title: Assign IDs to new specifications
use_cases.description: Add stable document IDs to new Markdown files using the repository format.

use_cases.title: Trace references during code review
use_cases.description: Find the document behind an @see docid reference and review related metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Check document IDs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Shirushi skill to validate document IDs in this repository. Run the safest lint command first and summarize any issues.

prompt_templates.title: Find one document
prompt_templates.scenario: Look up metadata for a known document ID.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Shirushi to find document API-SPEC-2025-0001-G. Report the document path, title, type, and any reference warnings.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare new documents
prompt_templates.scenario: Assign IDs to new documentation files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Shirushi to assign doc_ids to new files under docs/. Show the commands you plan to run, then review the resulting Git diff.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a pull request
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review against the main branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Shirushi to lint this branch against origin/main with reference checks. Group findings by broken IDs, changed hashes, and missing references.

output_examples.input: Validate my branch before I open a pull request.
output_examples.output: The branch passes doc_id format checks. Two @see references need review because their target documents changed.
The document ID resolves to an API specification with frontmatter metadata. The result includes its repository path and current hash status.
New IDs were assigned using the configured component, kind, year, serial, and check digit rules. Review the Git diff before committing.

best_practices: Run lint before committing documentation changes.
Review Git diffs after assign or rehash commands.
Keep .shirushi.yml aligned with repository document types.
anti_patterns: Do not run mutating commands without checking the working tree first.
Do not treat check digits as cryptographic integrity protection.
Do not manually rewrite immutable doc_ids after publication.

faq.question: What does this skill help manage?
faq.answer: It helps manage immutable document IDs, document metadata, hashes, and @see docid references in Git repositories.

faq.question: Does it install Shirushi?
faq.answer: No. The skill assumes the shirushi command is already installed and available in the repository environment.

faq.question: Can it change files?
faq.answer: Yes. Commands such as assign and rehash can modify repository files, so review diffs after running them.

faq.question: Does it require a specific file format?
faq.answer: The documented workflow uses YAML frontmatter in Markdown files and configuration in .shirushi.yml.

faq.question: Is the checksum a security feature?
faq.answer: No. The documented mod26AZ checksum is a document ID check digit, not cryptographic protection.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
